en: You write that a solid knowledge of the psychoanalytic literature is necessary for initiation into psychoanalysis. I should agree, but with a certain reservation: the more one reads, the more one notices how many contradictions there are among the different writers, and less and less does one know—until one has had sufficient personal experience—to which view to give adherence, since quite frequently assertions are made without any proof. For example, I had thought (strengthened in the view by my own experience of suggestion-therapy) that the transference to the doctor might be an essential condition in the patient's cure. But you write: "We psychoanalysts do not build upon the patient's faith, rather do we have to deal with his criticism." And Stekel writes, on the other hand ( Zentralblatt für Psychoanalyse , 3rd year, vol. IV., p. 176, "Ausgänge der psychoanalytischen Kuren"): "Love for the doctor can become a power essential to recovery. Neurotics never get well for love of themselves. They recover out of love for the doctor. They give him that pleasure." Here again, surely, stress is laid on the power of suggestion? And yet Stekel too thinks he is a psychoanalyst pure and simple. On the other hand, you say in your letter of Jan. 20th that "the doctor's personality is one of the main factors in the cure." Should not this expression be translated: "When the doctor inspires respect in the patient and is worthy of his love, the patient will gladly follow his example and endeavour to recover from his neurosis and fulfil his human duties in the widest sense"? I think one can only emerge from all this uncertainty by means of much personal experience, which will indicate also which way best suits one's own personality and brings the greatest therapeutic success. This is a further reason for undergoing analysis oneself, to recognise fully what one is. I was decidedly in agreement with your definition of psychoanalysis in its first (negative) portion: psychoanalysis is neither an anamnesis nor a method of examination after the fashion of a test for intelligence, nor yet a psychocatharsis . In your second (positive) part, however, your definition: "Psychoanalysis is a method of discovering the line of least resistance to the harmonious development of the whole personality," seems to me valid for the patient's inertia, but not for the releasing of the sublimated libido with a view to the new direction of life. You consider that the neurosis causes a lack of singleness of aim in life, because opposing tendencies hinder psychic adaptation. True, but will not this psychic adaptation eventuate quite differently according as the patient, when well, directs his life either to the avoidance of pain merely (line of least resistance) or to the achievement of the greatest pleasure?—In the first case he would be more passive, he would merely reconcile himself "to the emptiness of reality" (Stekel, loc. cit. , p. 187). In the second he would be "filled with enthusiasm" for something or other or some person or other. But what will determine this choice of his as to whether he will be passive rather than active in his "second life"? In your view, will the determining factor manifest itself spontaneously in the course of the analysis, and must the doctor carefully avoid swaying the balance to one side or other by his influence? Or must he, if he does not renounce the right to canalise the patient's libido in some particular direction, renounce the right to be called a psychoanalyst, and is he to be regarded as "moderate" or altogether as "wild"? [181] (Cf. Furtmüller, "Wandlungen in der Freudschen Schule," Zentralblatt für Psychoanalyse , vols. IV., V., 3rd year, p. 191.) But I think you have already answered this question, since in your last letter you write: "Every interference on the part of the analyst is a gross mistake in technique. So-called chance is the law and the order of psychoanalysis." But, torn from its context, perhaps this does not quite give your whole meaning. With regard to detailed explanation of the psychoanalytic method before the beginning of the analysis, I think you agree with Freud and Stekel: give too little rather than too much. For the knowledge instilled into a patient remains more or less half-knowledge, and half-knowledge engenders "the desire to know better" (than the analyst), which only impedes progress. So, after brief explanation, first "let the patient talk," then and there point out connexions, then after the exhaustion of the conscious material, take dreams.
zh: 您寫道，對精神分析文獻的紮實認識是進入精神分析領域的必要條件。我大致同意，但有所保留：一個人讀得越多，就越會注意到不同作者之間存在多少矛盾，而越發無所適從——直到擁有足夠的個人經驗——才知道該贊同哪種觀點，因為許多論斷常常毫無根據地提出。例如，我曾認為（並因自身在暗示療法中的經驗而強化了此觀點）對醫生的移情可能是患者康復的必要條件。但您寫道：「我們精神分析師並非建立在患者的信心之上，反而必須處理他的批判。」另一方面，史德克爾卻寫道（《精神分析中央期刊》，第三年，第四卷，第176頁，〈精神分析療程的出路〉）：「對醫生的愛可以成為康復所必需的力量。神經症患者從不會為了愛自己而痊癒。他們是出於對醫生的愛而康復。他們為了取悅醫生而康復。」這裡，難道不又是強調了暗示的力量嗎？然而史德克爾也認為自己是個不折不扣的精神分析師。另一方面，您在1月20日的信中說：「醫生的人格是治療中的主要因素之一。」這句話是否應翻譯為：「當醫生贏得患者的尊重並值得他的愛時，患者會樂於效法他的榜樣，努力從神經症中康復，並在最廣泛的意義上履行其人性義務」？我認為，唯有透過大量的個人經驗，才能從這一切不確定性中脫身，而這些經驗也將指出哪條路最適合自己的個性，並帶來最大的治療成功。這是接受自我分析、充分認識自己的另一個理由。我完全同意您對精神分析定義的第一部分（否定部分）：精神分析既不是一種病史採集，也不是一種類似智力測驗的檢查方法，更不是一種精神宣洩。然而，在您的第二部分（肯定部分）中，您的定義：「精神分析是一種發現通往整全人格和諧發展之最小阻力線的方法」，在我看來，這對患者的惰性有效，但對於釋放昇華的力比多以導向新的生活方向則不然。您認為神經症導致人生目標缺乏一致性，因為對立的傾向阻礙了心理適應。確實如此，但這種心理適應的結果，難道不會因患者康復後將其生活導向僅僅是避免痛苦（最小阻力線），還是導向獲得最大快樂，而截然不同嗎？——在第一種情況下，他會更被動，僅僅是使自己「順應現實的空虛」（史德克爾，同上，第187頁）。在第二種情況下，他會對某事或某人「充滿熱情」。然而，是什麼決定了他這種選擇——在他「第二段人生」中究竟是消極還是積極？在您看來，這個決定因素是否會在分析過程中自發顯現，而醫生必須謹慎避免以自己的影響力將天平傾向任何一邊？或者，如果他不放棄將患者的力比多引導至特定方向的權利，就必須放棄被稱為精神分析師的權利，並被視為「溫和派」或乾脆是「野蠻派」？[181]（參見福特穆勒，〈佛洛伊德學派的演變〉，《精神分析中央期刊》，第四、五卷，第三年，第191頁。）但我認為您已經回答了這個問題，因為您在上一封信中寫道：「分析師的任何干預都是技術上的嚴重錯誤。所謂的偶然性，正是精神分析的法則與秩序。」然而，脫離上下文，這或許並未完全表達您的全部意思。關於分析開始前對精神分析方法的詳細解釋，我認為您同意佛洛伊德和史德克爾的觀點：寧可給得太少，也不要給得太多。因為灌輸給患者的知識或多或少仍是半知半解，而半知半解會滋生「想要比分析師知道得更好」的慾望，這只會阻礙進展。因此，在簡短解釋之後，先「讓患者說話」，然後當場指出其中的關聯，接著在耗盡意識材料之後，再處理夢境。
